questions.question: What is the key difference between compassion fatigue and burnout?

options.text: They are the same thing with different names
Compassion fatigue is secondary traumatic stress from absorbing others' distress (can be sudden); burnout is chronic exhaustion from overcommitment (develops gradually)
Compassion fatigue only happens to healthcare workers
Burnout is more serious than compassion fatigue

questions.explanation: Compassion fatigue is secondary traumatic stress from absorbing others' suffering—it can develop suddenly and involves intrusive thoughts, hypervigilance, and emotional numbness. Burnout is chronic exhaustion from sustained overcommitment—it develops gradually and involves exhaustion, cynicism, and reduced efficacy. They are distinct syndromes that can coexist.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'other-oriented empathy' and 'self-oriented empathy' (over-identification)?

options.text: There is no difference—empathy is empathy
Other-oriented empathy means witnessing their pain without absorbing it (sustainable); self-oriented empathy means absorbing their pain as if it's yours (leads to compassion fatigue)
Self-oriented empathy is healthier because you care more
Other-oriented empathy means you don't really care about them

questions.explanation: Other-oriented empathy means you witness and feel compassion FOR them without merging with their distress—you stay grounded in your own emotional state. Self-oriented empathy (over-identification) means you absorb their pain as if it's happening to you—you merge emotional states. Research shows self-oriented empathy is a key risk factor for compassion fatigue.

questions.question: Why is caregiver self-care described as 'an ethical imperative, not a luxury'?

options.text: Because caregivers deserve treats and rewards
Because taking care of yourself protects your capacity to provide quality care—you can't pour from an empty cup
Because self-care prevents all caregiver stress
Because caregivers are more important than care recipients

questions.explanation: Self-care in caregiving is ethical because depleted caregivers provide lower-quality care, experience higher burnout rates, and eventually collapse—which harms the care recipient. Research shows caregivers with sustainable boundaries provide better care and sustain caregiving longer. Protecting yourself protects the person you're caring for.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of a healthy caregiver role boundary?

options.text: Doing all caregiving tasks yourself to prove you're a good caregiver
Recognizing when a task requires professional expertise and arranging appropriate support: 'I can provide emotional support. I can't provide medical care—you need a professional for that.'
Refusing to help with any physical tasks
Making all decisions for the care recipient without their input

questions.explanation: A healthy role boundary involves recognizing the limits of your capacity and expertise, then arranging appropriate professional support. This ensures the care recipient gets the best care while protecting you from burnout. It's not about refusing to help—it's about ensuring the right person provides the right support.

questions.question: When should a caregiver seek professional support (therapy, support groups, respite care)?

options.text: Only when they're completely collapsed and can't function
Never—asking for help means you're weak

When experiencing compassion fatigue symptoms, burnout symptoms, chronic exhaustion that doesn't improve with rest, resentment affecting the relationship, or social isolation—professional support is essential, not optional
Only if the care recipient gives permission

questions.explanation: Research shows caregivers who access professional support experience lower burnout rates, better mental health, and sustained caregiving capacity. Professional help is essential when you're experiencing compassion fatigue, burnout, health deterioration, relationship-damaging resentment, or isolation. Asking for help is wisdom, not weakness.

title: Caring Without Losing Yourself — Caregiving Boundaries - Quiz
